title: Vegetarian Sausage Roll
subtitle: Puff pastry filled with a mushroom, lentil, and cheese mixture that bakes to a satisfying bite
excerpt: A vegetarian sausage roll needs a filling with enough fat and texture to stand in for the pork. Chestnut mushrooms, green lentils, cheddar, and herbs give a filling that has both substance and flavour, and holds together when sliced or bitten.
sourceNotes: British vegetarian adaptation of the traditional pork sausage roll. Yotam Ottolenghi, Plenty (2010): mushroom-based savoury filling techniques. Contemporary British vegetarian baking practice.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze unbaked rolls for up to 2 months. Bake from frozen at 200°C for 30-35 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Make a double batch and freeze half unbaked. They bake straight from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The filling can be made a day ahead. Assemble and chill on the day of baking.
recipe.temperatureNote: high throughout for maximum pastry rise

glossaryTerms.definition: A French preparation of finely chopped mushrooms cooked down in butter until all moisture has evaporated, forming a concentrated, dry paste. The technique is also the basis for vegetarian sausage roll fillings: mushrooms provide umami and fat absorption.
Beaten egg brushed over raw pastry before baking to produce a glossy, golden colour. Essential for sausage rolls: it distinguishes a proper bake from a pale, doughy result.

content.text: The problem with most vegetarian sausage rolls is water. Mushrooms are 90% water; if you do not cook that water out before rolling them in pastry, the moisture will leak into the puff pastry and prevent it from rising. The solution is a
method: cook the mushrooms over high heat until the pan is dry and the mushrooms have reduced to a concentrated, dark paste.
The lentils provide protein and bind the filling. The cheddar melts slightly and holds the roll together once it sets. An
is what makes the difference between a pale roll and a golden one.
in a large frying pan over high heat. Add the onion and garlic, cook for 5 minutes, then add the
and cook over high heat, stirring frequently, for 10-12 minutes until all moisture has evaporated and the mushrooms are dry and concentrated. Off the heat, stir in the drained
, thyme leaves, and Worcestershire sauce if using. Season with
Unroll or lay out each sheet of the

on a lightly floured surface. Divide the filling into two portions and spread each one in a log along the centre third of each sheet. Fold the pastry over the filling and press the seam to seal. Brush with beaten egg and score the surface lightly. Cut each log into 6 rolls and place on a lined baking tray, seam-down. Chill for 20 minutes.
Heat the oven to 200°C (180°C fan). Bake for 22-25 minutes until deeply golden and puffed. Rest for 5 minutes before serving.

rows.symptom: Pastry is soggy at the base
rows.cause: Mushroom filling was not cooked dry enough, or the filling was still warm when rolled
rows.fix: Cook the mushrooms until the pan is completely dry and the sound changes from a sizzle to a gentle spit. Cool completely before rolling.

rows.cause: Too little cheese to bind, or lentils were too wet
rows.fix: Drain tinned lentils thoroughly and pat dry. Extra cheese (20-30 g more) helps bind.

rows.cause: Egg wash too thin or pastry was warm when it went in
rows.fix: Use undiluted beaten egg. Pastry must be cold.

rows.symptom: Pastry tears when rolling around the filling
rows.cause: Too much filling, or pastry was too thin
rows.fix: Leave at least 2 cm of clear pastry on each side of the filling log for the overlap seam.

content.text: Vegetarian sausage rolls became a staple of the British bakery and party table in the 2010s as plant-based eating moved from specialist shops to the mainstream. The filling needs to be satisfying enough to stand alongside its pork counterpart, and a dry mushroom and lentil base achieves this. These are party food, picnic food, and children's tea food in equal measure.
